channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Oil Companies’ Huge Profits Revive Calls for Temporary Windfall Taxes
item.description: European nations imposed temporary taxes in the 2022 energy shock when Russia invaded Ukraine, but whether they can effectively help households is up for debate.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Exxon Mobil refinery in Texas. The United States has not been hit as hard by energy price increases as countries in Asia and Europe.

item.title: Why Countries Are Stocking Up on Gold
item.description: As risk has escalated, central banks have bought more gold to stash in reserve. A widening Middle East war could add to the urgency.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

item.title: Where in the World Is All That Gold Stored?
item.description: As central banks buy more gold, where to put all that heavy metal is an increasingly important question. Reserves must be secure and ready to trade in a crisis.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: More than 60 central banks store gold at the Bank of England.

item.title: Crop Undercount Raises Questions About Reliability of U.S.D.A. Data
item.description: Corn estimates were off by 4.5 million acres last year. A lack of survey responses, not job cuts, led to the miss, the Agriculture Department said.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.media:credit: Kathryn Gamble for The New York Times
item.media:description: A cornfield in Earlham, Iowa, last fall. The Agriculture Department botched its estimate for the size of the 2025 corn crop.

item.title: Behind Powell’s High-Stakes Decision to Stay at the Fed
item.description: Jerome H. Powell will remain a governor at the Federal Reserve after his term as chair ends, in a bid to guard against a further incursion by the Trump administration on the central bank’s independence.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell on Wednesday during what is expected to be his final news conference as chair of the Federal Reserve.

item.title: U.S. Economy Grew 2 Percent in Early 2026 Even as War in Iran Began to Hit Energy Prices
item.description: Gross domestic product expanded at a 2 percent annual rate in the first three months of the year, a period including first weeks of conflict in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. economy continued to prove resilient in the first three months of the year, even as energy prices soared in the early weeks of the war.

item.title: How Powell Just Complicated Trump’s Fed Plans
item.description: Traders now think the Federal Reserve will keep steady interest rates, or perhaps even increase them, amid succession intrigue at the central bank.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jay Powell has made an extraordinary decision to stay on at the central bank after his tenure as chair expires next month.

item.title: Casa, a Handyman Start-Up, Aims to Automate Home Maintenance
item.description: Casa, a company founded by former Uber executives, says it uses artificial intelligence and a stable of handymen to take care of members’ homes.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael Mizrahi, left, and Michael York founded Casa after working at Uber and CloudKitchens, the virtual restaurant company owned by Travis Kalanick.

item.title: Senators Say Lawyers Helped Wealthy Clients Dodge Taxes in Puerto Rico
item.description: Rich Americans are writing off taxes they owed before they moved to Puerto Rico, the Senate Finance Committee says.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Drucker
item.media:credit: Xavier Garcia/Bloomberg
item.media:description: Dan Morehead, founder of the cryptocurrency investment firm Pantera Capital. Shortly after he relocated to Puerto Rico in 2021, his company sold a large position, generating capital gains of more than $1 billion.

item.title: Palantir Is Making a French Chore Coat. Yes, That Palantir.
item.description: The data analysis company is selling a version of the jacket that it says will show its commitment to “re-industrializing America.”
item.dc:creator: Yola Mzizi
item.media:credit: Palantir
item.media:description: Palantir’s new chore coat comes in bright blue and black.

item.title: With the Arrival of Goop Kitchen, Is New York Food Going Full L.A.?
item.description: Gwyneth Paltrow’s healthy-eating enterprise is just one of several recent Los Angeles exports to a rival city with its own ideas about what’s good.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin
item.media:credit: Emma McIntyre/Getty Images
item.media:description: The mission of Goop Kitchen, according to Gwyneth Paltrow, is to bring healthy food to a mass audience. Last week, New York City became its first market outside California.

item.title: Oil Price Hits Wartime High Above $120 a Barrel as Iran War Standoff Continues
item.description: Strong earnings and economic data buck fears that prolonged disruption to Middle East fuel supplies will eventually dent economic growth.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison and Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images

item.title: Rising Fuel Prices Force Policymakers to Weigh Excruciating Choices
item.description: The Bank of England and European Central Bank held interest rates steady on Thursday, as officials search for signs of possible longer-term damage and warn of the impact of a prolonged energy shock.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gas station in Paris. The war in Iran had an almost immediate impact on European inflation, increasing gasoline prices as well as airfares and other fuel-intensive activities.

item.title: Roger Sweet, Creator of the He-Man Action Figure, Dies at 91
item.description: The musclebound character he developed as a toy designer for Mattel gave rise to the Masters of the Universe franchise and helped define the machismo of the 1980s.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb

item.title: Why the U.A.E. Is Quitting OPEC
item.description: The United Arab Emirates is walking away from OPEC this May. The New York Times’ energy reporter, Rebecca Elliott, breaks down how the war with Iran provided the perfect opening for the Emirates to go solo.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott, Nour Idriss, Jon Miller, Stephanie Swart and Paul Abowd
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries

item.title: Ford Reports Higher Profit Thanks Partly to Tariff Refund
item.description: Ford Motor said it expected the federal government to refund $1.3 billion the company paid in tariffs that the Supreme Court later struck down.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:credit: Joe Raedle/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ford Motor vehicles at a dealership in Miami.

item.title: Takeaways from Powell’s final news conference as Fed chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: Meta Deal Reversal Deepens Split Between China and Silicon Valley
item.description: Beijing’s insistence that Meta unwind its deal with a Chinese A.I. start-up escalates the geopolitical fight over advanced tech.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Erin Griffith
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: China demanded on Monday that Meta’s $2 billion deal with the A.I. start-up Manus be undone.

item.title: Takeaways from Powell’s final Fed meeting as chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: Powell Says He Will Stay On as Fed Governor After Term as Chair Ends
item.description: Jerome H. Powell cited lingering legal threats against him and the Federal Reserve in explaining his decision to remain at the central bank.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The announcement ends months of speculation about whether Jerome H. Powell would choose to continue on as a member of the Board of Governors at the Federal Reserve.

item.title: Bill Ackman’s I.P.O., Pershing Square USA, Raises $5 Billion
item.description: The initial public offering of Mr. Ackman’s new fund, Pershing Square USA, raised $5 billion.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Farrell and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Brendan Mcdermid/Reuters
item.media:description: Bill Ackman, founder and chief executive of Pershing Square, at the New York Stock Exchange.

item.title: World Bank estimates energy prices will surge 24 percent in 2026.
item.description: Iran war is fueling inflation and slowing growth around the world, according to a new report.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport

item.title: How the Fed’s rate decision affects your finances.
item.description: The central bank’s interest rate policy influences car and home loans, credit cards, savings accounts and more.
item.dc:creator: Tara Siegel Bernard
item.media:credit: Sean Rayford for The New York Times
item.media:description: Some home loans are closely tethered to the Federal Reserve’s rate decisions.

item.title: United Airlines CEO, Rebuffed by American, Says He’s Skeptical of Other Deals
item.description: The chief executive, Scott Kirby, said that buying a smaller airline might not be worth the effort.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: “Doing a merger takes a lot of calories and a lot of energy,” Scott Kirby, chief executive of United Airlines, said in an interview.

item.title: Warsh Clears an Early Hurdle on Path to Fed Chair
item.description: The Senate Banking Committee advanced Kevin Warsh’s nomination as Fed chair on Wednesday, teeing up the full chamber to hold a vote soon.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, President Trump’s nominee for Federal Reserve chair.

item.title: Uber Expands to Hotel Bookings With Expedia Partnership
item.description: In partnership with Expedia, the ride-booking, food-delivering, package-transporting service takes another step toward becoming a super app.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.media:credit: Ashley Gilbertson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Park Lane Hotel in New York is one of the lodging options that can be booked through Uber.

item.title: What’s happening at the Federal Reserve today.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: Fidelity, Vanguard Won’t Let Donor-Advised Fund Holders Donate to Southern Poverty Law Center
item.description: Both entities cited the Justice Department investigation into the civil rights organization in messages to its charitable account holders.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber

item.title: The Stakes of Trump’s Latest Fight With Disney
item.description: The Federal Communications Commission is clashing again with the media giant. It’s another warning about the costs of angering President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brendan Carr, the chairman of the Federal Communications Commission, has made it clear that he will investigate media companies over perceived bias.

item.title: U.S. Gas Prices Climb Further as Effects of Iran War Reverberate
item.description: Crude oil prices hit a fresh high since the start of the war in Iran, as energy supplies from the Middle East remain disrupted.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: New Disney Boss Is Tested by Trump and His Administration
item.description: Josh D’Amaro, who took the helm in February, will have to lead Disney through surprising new scrutiny from the president.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes and John Koblin
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Disney’s new chief executive, Josh D’Amaro, at Disneyland last year, when he was chairman of Disney experiences.

item.title: Loss of Emirates Further Weakens OPEC’s Influence
item.description: The exit of the United Arab Emirates is the most significant in a series of departures from the oil cartel in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: Oil storage in Big Spring, Texas. OPEC is less powerful than it once was, in no small part because of the rise of U.S. oil production.

item.title: Judge Asks Justice Department: Will You Oppose Trump?
item.description: A federal judge has ordered the Justice Department to explain whether it intends to contest President Trump’s $10 billion lawsuit against the I.R.S. over the disclosure of his tax returns.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump sued the Internal Revenue Service over the leak of his tax returns to news media outlets, arguing that the agency should have done more to prevent the disclosures.

item.title: Fed Meeting Underscores Tough Task Ahead for Warsh
item.description: Jerome H. Powell on Wednesday announced he would stay on as a governor at the central bank as internal divisions sharpen about the policy path forward.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell on Wednesday said that he would remain at the central bank after his term as chair ends on May 15 and his successor, Kevin M. Warsh, takes over.

item.title: 5 Hotels for Your Next U.S. Road Trip
item.description: Add these new or newly revamped accommodations to your summer driving itinerary.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Rosenbloom

item.title: In Backlash Against Tech in Schools, Parents Are Winning Rollbacks
item.description: From Salt Lake City to New York City, parents are demanding more sway over the digital tools that schools give children.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Singer
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sandra Martinez Roe, with her son, is a leader of a Los Angeles parents’ group pushing the district to audit its technology contracts and limit the use of tech in schools.

item.title: Meta Charged With Failing to Keep Children Off Instagram and Facebook in Europe
item.description: European Union regulators said the company did not have effective controls to check a user’s self-declared date of birth, in violation of an online safety law.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Regulators in Europe said Meta appears to be violating the Digital Services Act, an E.U. law passed in 2022 to force social media companies to police their platforms more aggressively.

item.title: Starbucks Reports Strong Store Traffic and Rise in Quarterly Sales
item.description: The chain’s chief executive credited its turnaround plan for improved U.S. same-store sales, but said higher gas and utility costs were a concern.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.media:credit: Nico Schinco for The New York Times
item.media:description: Global sales for Starbucks stores open at least a year rose 6.2 percent in the latest quarter compared with a year earlier.

item.title: Prime Minister Mark Carney Says Canada’s Economy Is Expected to Grow and Deficit to Fall
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney’s budget update keeps to his path of diversifying away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Sean Kilpatrick/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving at Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Tuesday.

item.title: Deal Talks Between Pernod and Maker of Jack Daniel’s Falter
item.description: The collapse of the talks could pave the way for another spirits company, Sazerac, to open negotiations with Brown-Forman.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images
item.media:description: Brown-Forman, which makes Jack Daniel’s whiskey, and Pernod Ricard did not give reasons for the failure of their merger talks.

item.title: Treasury Issues More Sanctions on Iranian Oil Exports
item.description: The measures aim to crack down on Iran’s shadow banking system and Chinese purchases of Iranian oil.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: “Iran’s shadow banking system serves as a critical financial lifeline for its armed forces, enabling activities that disrupt global trade and fuel violence across the Middle East,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement.

item.title: What Is OPEC, and How Does the Oil Cartel Influence Petroleum Prices?
item.description: The group of oil-producing countries supplied more than 25 percent of the world’s oil before the war in Iran. Its members have influenced energy markets through the years.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Christian Bruna/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Vienna headquarters of the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, which was created in 1960 as a way to control oil policies and stabilize global markets.

item.title: Supreme Court Appears Skeptical of Falun Gong Lawsuit Against Cisco
item.description: The court’s decision could have broader implications for lawsuits seeking to hold companies liable for international human rights abuses.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Jonah Markowitz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of Falun Gong during a protest meditation in Manhattan in 2024. The Chinese government has banned the group.

item.title: FCC Orders a Review of ABC’s Licenses Amid Feud Between Trump and Kimmel
item.description: The agency said the review was related to the network’s diversity and inclusion policies. But it came amid a fight between the president and the network’s late night host, Jimmy Kimmel.
item.dc:creator: Jim Rutenberg and John Koblin
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brendan Carr, the chairman of the Federal Communications Commission, has repeatedly threatened to take action against broadcasters.

item.title: For Many Pro Athletes, Post-Career Financial Worries Loom Large
item.description: The tennis player Mackenzie McDonald has earned well up to this point, but high expenses and a short career timeline have prompted him, and others, to search for a backup plan.
item.dc:creator: Cindy Shmerler
item.media:credit: Michael Stravato for The New York Times
item.media:description: At 31, Mackenzie McDonald has earned more than $7 million in prize money. But his recent demotion in ranking has him thinking about his financial future. “I’m very aware of my post-career life,” he said.

item.title: First Fully Loaded L.N.G. Tanker Since War Began Appears to Have Crossed the Strait
item.description: Liquefied natural gas transit has been effectively halted. A ship managed by Abu Dhabi’s state oil company managed to cross, said Kpler, a maritime data firm.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Andrew Matthews/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Before the war, about 20 percent of the world’s liquefied natural gas originated in the Middle East, virtually all of it headed to Asia.

item.title: G.M.’s Profit Bolstered by a $500 Million Tariff Refund
item.description: Because of a February Supreme Court decision, General Motors said it would receive refunds on some of the tariffs it had paid.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A General Motors plant in Spring Hill, Tenn. The company is still affected by other tariffs — including those on imported steel, aluminum, cars and auto parts.

item.title: If OpenAI Is Missing User and Revenue Targets, Are They Falling Behind the AI Race?
item.description: The artificial intelligence giant has reportedly fallen behind on its own user and revenue targets, raising questions about its data center and I.P.O. plans
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Vivienne Walt
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Altman, OpenAI’s C.E.O.

item.title: Despite Trump’s Demands, His Fed Pick Is Unlikely to Get a Quick Rate Cut
item.description: The Federal Reserve is expected to hold interest rates steady this week as Jerome H. Powell presides over what is likely to be his last meeting as chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: It is unclear whether Jerome H. Powell will leave the Federal Reserve once his successor as chair is confirmed. His term as governor runs until early 2028.
